title: One-Shot Normal Mode
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-O} runs one *normal mode* command without leaving *insert mode*.
summary: {key:Ctrl-O} from *insert mode* lets you fire one *normal mode* command and snap right back to insertion. The pinnacle of stay-in-flow editing.

blocks.text: {key:Ctrl-O} from *insert mode* runs exactly one *normal mode* command, then drops you back into *insert mode* at the new cursor position. It's the express elevator between modes.

rows: Delete inner word, stay in *insert mode*

blocks.title: Why It Counts as One Command
blocks.text:
The *insert mode* {key:Ctrl-O} sets an internal flag (`restart_edit`) that says: *finish exactly one normal-mode operation, then resume insert mode at the cursor*. Operator + motion still counts as one — {key:d}{key:i}{key:w} is one command. But {key:.} (dot-repeat) executed via {key:Ctrl-O} replays whatever the previous Normal command was, which can surprise you.

Note: any *insert mode* change you've already made *up to this point* is committed to the undo tree as a single edit. The next character you type starts a fresh undo unit.
{key:Ctrl-O} is what makes Vim's *insert mode* bearable. Don't fight to stay in Insert — pop out for one command and come back.
